questions.text: The element found maximum in the soil layer is
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: A) Oxygen** ✅

**Question Explanation for DRDO CEPTAM-11 STA-B Tier-I (General Science – Chemistry/Soil Science)**
This is a **straight 1-mark MCQ** from **NCERT Class 9 Science Chapter 14 (Natural Resources)** and **Earth’s Crust Composition** (also in Lucent General Science – Environment section).

**Exact Fact (Write this in exam):**
The **element found maximum in the soil layer** (and Earth’s crust) is **Oxygen** (about 46–49 % by weight).
Soil is formed by weathering of rocks (Earth’s crust). Oxygen is present in the form of silicates, oxides, carbonates, etc.
Silicon is the **second** most abundant element (~27–28 %).

### All Options Explained (Very Important – Examiner mixes to confuse)

| Option | Element | % in Soil/Earth’s Crust | Correct or Wrong? | Why? (Exam Point) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | **Oxygen** | ~46–49 % | ✅ **Correct** | Most abundant element. Present in almost all soil minerals (SiO₂, Al₂O₃, Fe₂O₃ etc.). Standard answer in DRDO/SSC exams. |
| **B** | Nitrogen | ~0.1 % or less | ❌ Wrong | Nitrogen is abundant in air (78 %), not in soil. Only small amount in organic matter. |
| **C** | Manganese | Trace (0.1 % or less) | ❌ Wrong | Micronutrient only – very less quantity. |
| **D** | Silicon | ~27–28 % | ❌ Wrong | Second most abundant (in sand/quartz), but **less than oxygen**. |

**Quick Trick to Remember (Exam Favourite):**

• **Oxygen** = King of Earth’s crust & soil (46 %)
• **Silicon** = Prince (28 %)
• Remember: “O > Si > Al > Fe” (most abundant order)
• Soil = Weathered crust → same composition.

### Possible Questions in Tier-I (Same Pattern)

1. Most abundant element in Earth’s crust / soil is: → Oxygen
2. Second most abundant element in soil is: → Silicon
3. The element present in maximum amount in soil layer is: → Oxygen
4. Which element is maximum in Earth’s crust? → Oxygen (options same as above)
5. Most abundant element in rocks/soil: → Oxygen

questions.text: Which one of the following is present in the largest amount in terms of percent by mass in the Earth’s crust?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Oxygen**

Oxygen forms a large number of minerals such as oxides and silicates.

Option Analysis:
• Silicon → Second most abundant.
• Oxygen → Correct; present mainly as oxides.
• Carbon → Mostly in organic matter and carbonate rocks.
• Calcium → Present in limestone but much smaller proportion.

questions.text: Which is the most abundant element after oxygen?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Silicon**

Silicon occurs in the form of **silicates and quartz (SiO₂)**.

Option Analysis:
• Silicon → Correct; second most abundant element.
• Carbon → Less abundant in crust.
• Sodium → Occurs in feldspar minerals.
• Chlorine → Mostly present in sea water.

questions.text: Which of the following is the most common element in the Universe?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: A) Hydrogen** ✅

**Question Explanation for DRDO CEPTAM-11 STA-B Tier-I (General Science – Chemistry/ Universe Composition)**
This is a **straight 1-mark MCQ** from **NCERT Class 9 Science Chapter 14 (Natural Resources)** and **Class 8 Chapter 10 (Reaching the Age of Adolescence)** or Lucent General Science – “Composition of Universe”.

**Exact Fact (Write this in exam):**
The **most common element in the Universe** is **Hydrogen**.
It makes up about **73–75 % of the total mass** of the Universe.
Most of it is present in stars (like our Sun) and interstellar gas clouds.
Hydrogen is the simplest element (1 proton + 1 electron) and was the first element formed after the Big Bang.

### All Options Explained (Very Important – Examiner mixes Earth vs Universe facts)

| Option | Element | % in Universe (by mass) | Correct or Wrong? | Why? (Exam Point) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | **Hydrogen** | ~73–75 % | ✅ **Correct** | Most abundant element in the entire Universe. Standard answer in all defence/SSC exams. |
| **B** | Oxygen | ~1 % | ❌ Wrong | Oxygen is most abundant in **Earth’s crust/soil** (46 %), not in the Universe. |
| **C** | Nitrogen | ~0.02 % | ❌ Wrong | Nitrogen is abundant in **Earth’s atmosphere** (78 %), not in the Universe. |
| **D** | Carbon | ~0.5 % | ❌ Wrong | Carbon is important for life but very less in the Universe. |

**Quick Trick to Remember (Exam Favourite):**

• **Universe** = Hydrogen (75 %) + Helium (24 %)
• **Earth’s Crust** = Oxygen (46 %)
• **Earth’s Atmosphere** = Nitrogen (78 %)

Remember: “Big Bang → Hydrogen first → Most common in Universe”.

### Possible Questions in Tier-I (Same Pattern)

1. Most abundant element in the Universe is: → Hydrogen
2. Second most abundant element in the Universe is: → Helium
3. Most abundant element in Earth’s crust is: → Oxygen (link with previous soil question)
4. Which element was formed first after Big Bang? → Hydrogen
5. Hydrogen is the lightest / simplest element – True or False?

questions.text: Approximately, how many different chemical elements exist in the Earth?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: D) 100** ✅

**Question Explanation for DRDO CEPTAM-11 STA-B Tier-I (General Science – Chemistry)**
This is a **straight 1-mark MCQ** from **Lucent General Science / Examveda / Careericons** type books (very common in DRDO, SSC, Railway Tier-I papers).

**Exact Answer (Write this in exam):**
Approximately **100 different chemical elements** exist in the Earth.
(Out of 118 total known elements in the periodic table, about **94** occur naturally on Earth, and the rest are synthetic. For competitive exams, they take the approximate round figure of **100**.)

### All Options Explained (Very Important – Examiner tests approximation)

| Option | Number | Correct or Wrong? | Why? (Exam Point) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 300 | ❌ Wrong | Too high – even total known elements are only 118 |
| **B** | 250 | ❌ Wrong | Still way higher |
| **C** | 200 | ❌ Wrong | Still higher than actual |
| **D** | **100** | ✅ **Correct** | Standard answer in all Tier-I MCQ books for “elements in the Earth” |

**Quick Trick to Remember (Exam Favourite):**

• Total elements in Periodic Table = **118**
• Naturally occurring in Earth ≈ **94** → **Exam answer = 100** (approximate)

Remember: “Earth has roughly **100** elements”.

**Link with Previous Questions:**

• Most abundant element in **Universe** = Hydrogen
• Most abundant in **Earth’s crust/soil** = Oxygen
• Total different elements in **Earth** ≈ **100**

**Possible Questions in Tier-I (Same Pattern)**

1. Total number of elements in periodic table is: → 118
2. Number of naturally occurring elements on Earth is approximately: → 100 (or 94 in advanced papers)
3. How many elements exist in the Earth? → 100

questions.text: Which of the following is a fundamental element?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Diamond**

Diamond is an allotrope of **pure carbon**, which is a chemical element.

Option Analysis:
• Sand → Mainly silicon dioxide compound.
• Diamond → Correct; pure carbon.
• Marble → Calcium carbonate rock.
• Sugar → Organic compound (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁).

questions.text: The chemical composition of diamond is
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Carbon**

Diamond is a crystalline allotrope of carbon.

Option Analysis:
• Carbon → Correct; arranged in tetrahedral crystal lattice.
• Nitrogen / Nickel / Zinc → Elements not forming diamond.

Keep going — DRDO CEPTAM-11 STA-B is yours! **Electron Affinity (EA)** – Super Simple Explanation for **DRDO CEPTAM-11 STA-B Tier-I** (General Science – Chemistry)

### Exact Definition (Write this word-for-word in exam)
**Electron affinity** is the **amount of energy released** when an **extra electron** is added to a **neutral gaseous atom** to form a negative ion.

**Simple Equation (Memorize):**
X(g) + e⁻ → X⁻(g) + Energy (released)

• It measures how strongly an atom attracts an extra electron.
• Unit: kJ/mol (or eV/atom)
• Value is **negative** (energy is released → exothermic process)

### Why it is Important (Exam Point)
Higher (more negative) electron affinity → Atom has more tendency to gain electron → Forms stable negative ion easily.
Example: Halogens (Cl, F) have very high EA → form Cl⁻, F⁻ easily.

### Periodic Trends (Most Important – Directly linked to your last Assertion-Reason question)

| Direction | Trend of Electron Affinity | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Across a period** (left to right) | **Increases** (becomes more negative) | Atomic size decreases, nuclear charge increases → stronger attraction for extra electron |
| **Down a group** (top to bottom) | **Decreases** (becomes less negative) | Atomic size increases → extra electron is farther from nucleus → weaker attraction |

**Exception (Favourite in MCQs):**
Fluorine has slightly **lower** EA than Chlorine (due to small size → electron-electron repulsion in 2p orbital).

**Quick Trick to Remember:**

• “Left to right → EA ↑↑” (like ionisation energy but opposite sign)
• “Top to bottom → EA ↓↓” (size ↑ → attraction ↓)
• Highest EA → Chlorine (among all elements)
• Lowest EA → Noble gases (almost zero)

### Possible Questions in Tier-I (Exact Pattern)

1. Electron affinity is the energy ________ when electron is added.
   a) Absorbed b) Released → **b**

2. Electron affinity generally ________ down a group.
   a) Increases b) Decreases → **b** (this was your last Assertion-Reason question)

3. Which element has highest electron affinity?
   a) F b) Cl c) Br d) I → **b) Cl**

4. Electron affinity of noble gases is:
   a) High b) Low/zero c) Negative d) Positive → **b**

5. Assertion-Reason type (exactly like your last question) will come again!

**Your Score Update:**

You are now perfect in:

✅ All previous 30+ topics
✅ **Electron Affinity** (definition + trends)

This completes the full **Periodic Table Trends** portion of Tier-I Chemistry!

questions.text:
Consider the following statements with reference to the periodic table of chemical elements.
1. Ionization potential gradually decreases along a period.
2. In a group of elements, electron affinity decreases as the atomic weight increases.
3. In a given period, electronegativity decreases as the atomic number increases.
Which of these statement(s) is/are correct?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Only 2**.

Explanation of statements:
• Statement 1 → Incorrect. Ionization energy increases across a period.
• Statement 2 → Correct. Electron affinity generally decreases down a group.
• Statement 3 → Incorrect. Electronegativity increases across a period.

questions.text: Which one of the following pairs of metals constitutes the lightest metal and heaviest metal, respectively?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Lithium and Osmium**.

Lithium is the lightest metal, while osmium has the highest density among metals.

questions.text: Which is the hardest in the following?
questions.solution:
**This question is based on the Mohs Scale of Hardness**, which measures how resistant a material is to scratching.

❓ **Question**

**Which is the hardest in the following?**

**Options:**
A. Diamond
B. Glass
C. Quartz
D. Platinum

✅ **Correct Answer: A — Diamond**

---

📊 **Mohs Hardness Scale (Important for Exams)**

The **Mohs scale** ranks minerals from **1 (softest)** to **10 (hardest)**.

**Material — Mohs Hardness**

Talc — 1
Gypsum — 2
Calcite — 3
Fluorite — 4
Apatite — 5
Feldspar — 6
Quartz — 7
Topaz — 8
Corundum — 9
Diamond — 10

👉 **Diamond is the hardest natural material.**

---

🔍 **Explanation of Each Option**

**A. Diamond ✅ (Correct)**

**Hardness:** 10 (highest on Mohs scale)

**Structure:** Strong **covalent bonding of carbon atoms**

Diamond can **scratch all other materials**.

📌 **Used in:**

• Cutting tools
• Drilling equipment
• Jewellery

---

**B. Glass**

**Hardness:** ~5.5

Glass is mainly made of **silica (SiO₂)**.

It **can be scratched by quartz or diamond**.

📌 **Example:**
Quartz sand can scratch glass.

---

**C. Quartz**

**Hardness:** 7

**Chemical formula:** SiO₂

Quartz is **harder than glass but softer than diamond**.

📌 **Commonly found in:**

• Sand
• Rocks
• Watch crystals

---

**D. Platinum**

**Hardness:** ~4–4.5

Platinum is a **soft metal** compared to minerals.

It can be **easily scratched**.

📌 **Used in:**

• Jewellery
• Catalysts
• Electronics

---

📊 **Comparison Table (Easy Revision)**

**Material — Type — Hardness**

Diamond — Mineral — 10
Quartz — Mineral — 7
Glass — Amorphous solid — ~5.5
Platinum — Metal — ~4

👉 **Therefore:**

**Diamond > Quartz > Glass > Platinum**

---

🎯 **Exam Trick (DRDO / SSC / Railway)**

Remember:

**Diamond = Hardest natural substance**

So whenever **diamond appears in hardness questions → choose diamond.**

---

✅ **Final Answer: Diamond**

---

If you want, I can also show you **10 similar DRDO CEPTAM Tier-1 questions on hardness, materials, and minerals** (these are frequently asked).

questions.text: Which of the following is the electron configuration of a metallic element?
questions.solution:
**This question tests a very common DRDO / SSC concept:**
**How to identify metals using electron configuration.**

---

**Question**

**Which of the following is the electron configuration of a metallic element?**

A. 2, 8
B. 2, 8, 7
C. 2, 8, 8
D. 2, 8, 8, 2

---

**Key Concept (Very Important for Exams)**

**Metals usually have 1–3 electrons in the outermost shell (valence shell).**

**Why?**

Metals easily **lose electrons** to form **positive ions (cations)**.

**Valence electrons — Type**

1–3 → **Metal**
4 → **Metalloid / borderline**
5–7 → **Non-metal**
8 → **Noble gas (inert)**

---

**Option-by-Option Explanation**

**A. 2, 8**

Total electrons = **10**

Element = **Neon**

Electron distribution:
K shell = 2
L shell = 8

Outer shell = **8 electrons**

This means the **octet is complete**, so the atom is **stable and inert**.

Type → **Noble gas (non-metal)**

❌ **Not a metal**

---

**B. 2, 8, 7**

Total electrons = **17**

Element = **Chlorine**

Outer shell electrons = **7**

Atoms with **7 valence electrons** tend to **gain 1 electron**, forming negative ions.

Example reaction:

Cl + e⁻ → Cl⁻

Type → **Non-metal (Halogen)**

❌ **Not a metal**

---

**C. 2, 8, 8**

Total electrons = **18**

Element = **Argon**

Outer shell electrons = **8**

This is again a **complete octet**, making the atom **chemically inert**.

Type → **Noble gas**

❌ **Not a metal**

---

**D. 2, 8, 8, 2**

Total electrons = **20**

Element = **Calcium (Ca)**

Outer shell electrons = **2**

Metals **lose electrons easily**:

Ca → Ca²⁺ + 2e⁻

Since it can easily **lose its two outer electrons**, it behaves like a **typical metal**.

Type → **Alkaline earth metal**

✅ **Correct Answer**

---

**Final Answer**

**D. 2, 8, 8, 2**

---

⚡ **Quick Shortcut for Exams (Very Useful)**

If the **last shell has:**

1 electron → **Alkali metal (Na, K)**

2 electrons → **Alkaline earth metal (Mg, Ca)**

8 electrons → **Noble gas**

So **2,8,8,2 → metal (Calcium)**.

---

If you want, I can also show you **3 extremely common DRDO trap questions from electron configuration** that confuse **90% of students**.

questions.text: Which is the most reactive metal?
questions.solution:
**This question checks your understanding of metal reactivity trends in the periodic table**, which is a very frequent topic in **DRDO CEPTAM Tier-1 General Science**.

---

**Question**

**Which is the most reactive metal?**

A. Sodium
B. Calcium
C. Iron
D. Potassium

✅ **Correct Answer: D. Potassium**

Now let’s analyze why.

---

**Core Concept (Very Important)**

Metal **reactivity depends on how easily the atom loses electrons**.

Metals react by **losing electrons to form positive ions**.

Example:

K → K⁺ + e⁻

The **easier the electron is lost → the more reactive the metal**.

---

**Periodic Trend to Remember**

For **alkali metals (Group 1)**:

**Reactivity increases down the group.**

**Order:**

Li < Na < K < Rb < Cs

**Reason:**

• Atomic size increases
• Outer electron becomes farther from nucleus
• Nuclear attraction becomes weaker
• Electron is lost more easily

Thus **potassium is more reactive than sodium**.

---

**Option-by-Option Explanation**

**A. Sodium (Na)**

Electron configuration:

2, 8, 1

Valence electrons = **1**

Sodium easily loses this electron:

Na → Na⁺ + e⁻

It is **highly reactive**, but **less reactive than potassium**.

❌ **Not the correct answer**

---

**B. Calcium (Ca)**

Electron configuration:

2, 8, 8, 2

Valence electrons = **2**

Calcium must lose **two electrons**:

Ca → Ca²⁺ + 2e⁻

This requires **more energy**, so it is **less reactive than alkali metals like Na and K**.

❌ **Not correct**

---

**C. Iron (Fe)**

Iron is a **transition metal**.

**Properties:**

• Multiple oxidation states
• Forms compounds slowly
• Does not react vigorously with water

Example:

Iron reacts with oxygen slowly → **rusting**.

Therefore, iron is **much less reactive than sodium or potassium**.

❌ **Not correct**

---

**D. Potassium (K)**

Electron configuration:

2, 8, 8, 1

Valence electrons = **1**

But the **outer electron is farther from the nucleus than in sodium**.

So **potassium loses its electron even more easily**.

**Reaction with water:**

2K + 2H₂O → 2KOH + H₂ + Heat

This reaction is **violent and may even produce flame**.

Therefore **potassium is more reactive than sodium**.

✅ **Correct Answer**

---

**Important Exam Memory Rule**

Among these metals:

**K > Na > Ca > Fe**

Reactivity **decreases in this order**.

---

⚡ **Quick DRDO Exam Trick**

If options contain **Na and K**, the answer is usually:

👉 **Potassium (K)**

Because it is **lower in Group 1 → more reactive**.

---

If you want, I can also show you the **standard metal reactivity series** that DRDO exams frequently ask:

**K, Na, Ca, Mg, Al, Zn, Fe, Cu, Ag, Au**.

Understanding that **single list can solve 10+ types of MCQs instantly**. For **DRDO CEPTAM-11 STA-B Tier-1**, one list you should memorize completely is the **Metal Reactivity Series**. Many MCQs come directly or indirectly from it.

---

**1️⃣ Standard Metal Reactivity Series**

From **most reactive → least reactive**

**K > Na > Ca > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Pb > Cu > Ag > Au**

This means:

• **Potassium** is very reactive
• **Gold** is very unreactive (**noble metal**)

---

**2️⃣ Easy Memory Trick (Very Useful for Exams)**

A common mnemonic:

**“Please Stop Calling Me A Zebra Instead Try Learning How Copper Saves Gold.”**

**Word — Metal**

Please → **Potassium (K)**
Stop → **Sodium (Na)**
Calling → **Calcium (Ca)**
Me → **Magnesium (Mg)**
A → **Aluminium (Al)**
Zebra → **Zinc (Zn)**
Instead → **Iron (Fe)**
Try → **Lead (Pb)**
Learning → **Copper (Cu)**
How → **Silver (Ag)**
Gold → **Gold (Au)**

---

**3️⃣ Important Concepts From Reactivity Series**

**1️⃣ Metals above hydrogen**

These metals can **displace hydrogen from acids**.

Example:

Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl₂ + H₂

Metals like:

**K, Na, Ca, Mg, Al, Zn, Fe**

---

**2️⃣ Metals below hydrogen**

These **cannot displace hydrogen from acids**.

Examples:

**Cu, Ag, Au**

That is why **copper does not react with dilute HCl**.

---

**3️⃣ Highly reactive metals**

Top metals react **violently with water**.

Example:

2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂

Metals reacting with **cold water**:

• **Potassium**
• **Sodium**
• **Calcium**

---

**4️⃣ Moderately reactive metals**

These react with **steam, not cold water**.

Examples:

• **Zinc**
• **Iron**

---

**5️⃣ Least reactive metals**

These **do not react with water or dilute acids**.

Examples:

• **Copper**
• **Silver**
• **Gold**

---

**4️⃣ Typical DRDO Exam MCQs**

**Question 1**

Which metal is the **most reactive**?

A. Na
B. Mg
C. Fe
D. K

✅ **Answer: Potassium (K)**

---

**Question 2**

Which metal is **least reactive**?

A. Cu
B. Fe
C. Au
D. Zn

✅ **Answer: Gold (Au)**

---

**Question 3**

Which metal **cannot displace hydrogen from dilute HCl**?

A. Zn
B. Fe
C. Mg
D. Cu

✅ **Answer: Copper**

Because **copper is below hydrogen in the reactivity series**.

---

**Question 4**

Which metal reacts **most vigorously with water**?

A. Sodium
B. Iron
C. Copper
D. Zinc

✅ **Answer: Sodium**

---

**5️⃣ One Line You Must Memorize**

**K > Na > Ca > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Pb > Cu > Ag > Au**

This **single line can solve many DRDO Tier-1 questions**.

---

If you want, I can also show you **5 very tricky MCQs on the reactivity series** that **DRDO/SSC examiners like to use to confuse students**.

questions.text: Which one of the following is the hardest metal?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Tungsten**.

Tungsten has extremely high hardness and melting point.

questions.text: Which one of the following materials is very hard and very ductile?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Tungsten**.

Tungsten combines hardness with ductility and is used in filaments.

questions.text: The heaviest natural element is
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Uranium**.

Uranium (atomic number 92) is the heaviest naturally occurring element.

questions.text: Heaviest metal of the following is made of
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Uranium**.

Among these metals, uranium has the highest atomic mass.

questions.text: Which of the following is lighter than water?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Sodium**.

Sodium floats on water because its density is less than water.

questions.text: The chemical structure of the pearl is
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Calcium Carbonate**.

Pearls are mainly composed of calcium carbonate crystals.

questions.text: The main constituents of pearl are
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Aragonite and Conchiolin**.

Pearls consist of microscopic layers of **aragonite crystals bound by conchiolin protein**.
